title: Step 2: After connecting to your cluster, run commands to verify the user has the read or write privileges you assigned them.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.body:
- You must deploy `M10` or larger Atlas clusters to enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)
  integration. LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) integration is an Atlas Enterprise feature.

- Atlas does not support authorizing database users in Okta Active
  Directory synchronization groups.

- Atlas does not support single sign-on integration for database
  users. To learn about single-sign on integration for the Atlas
  administrative web interface, see
  [Configure Federated Authentication](atlas-federated-authentication).

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To manage LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) users or groups, you must have
[Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) or [Project Owner](<Project Owner>) access to Atlas.

sections.heading: Configure OneLogin for LDAP Authentication
sections.body:
The following procedure configures OneLogin for authentication
with Atlas:

- To learn more about setting up the Okta LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) interface,   see the
  [Okta documentation](https://help.okta.com/en/prod/Content/Topics/Directory/LDAP-interface-main.htm).

- Note your `<okta-instance-id>`. You must provide it
  in several places during the configuration process.

  The instance name is located in the URL you use to sign in to your
  Okta account:

  ```sh
  https://<okta-instance-id>.admin.okta.com
  ```

* [Create a new Okta user](https://help.okta.com/en-us/Content/Topics/users-groups-profiles/usgp-add-users.htm)
  to use as the Atlas bind user. The bind user is an Okta
  user that you use to query the account and to authenticate
  database users' credentials when they connect to an Atlas
  database.

  > **Important:** Don't use your own user account for the bind user.

* Use the following template to determine the full Distinguished Name
  (DN) of your bind user:

  ```sh
  uid=<bind-user-email>,dc=<okta-instance-id>,dc=okta,dc=com
  ```

  For example, if your `<bind-user-email>` is
  `bind@example.com` and your `<okta-instance-id>` is
  `mdb-example`, your bind user's DN is:

  ```sh
  uid=bind@example.com,ou=users,dc=mdb-example,dc=okta,dc=com
  ```

If they don't exist already, create users in Okta that you want to
grant database access to:

- Navigate to your Okta People page.

- Click Add Person.

- Enter the user's details. Use email addresses for usernames.

  > **Note:** Avoid entering email addresses with plus symbols (`+`). The
  > Atlas LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) integration may encounter issues with email
  > addresses containing plus symbols.

- Click Save.

sections.heading: Configure Atlas for LDAP Authentication
sections.body:
The following procedure enables Atlas to authenticate database users from
OneLogin LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol):

> **Note:** You might incur additional costs when you enable this feature.
> See [Advanced Security](advanced-security).

You may list multiple servers separated by commas. You cannot use different ports.

Add a User to DN mapping similar to the following example to allow
clients to provide their email addresses instead of full DNs when they
connect to Atlas databases:

```sh
[
   {
       "match": "(.+)",
       "substitution": "uid={0},ou=users,dc=<okta_instance_id>,dc=okta,dc=com"
   }
]
```

You may provide self-signed certificates.

Wait for Atlas to deploy your changes. Atlas verifies that
your clusters can connect to, authenticate with, and query your LDAP
servers using the configuration details that you provided.

> **Note:** [Skip this step](configure-okta-authz) if you want to enable
> LDAP authorization.

Add users managed in the Okta LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) to Atlas.

- Click  Add New Database User.

- Click LDAP User.

- Perform one of the following:

  - If you have not entered a User to DN Mapping, enter
    the full DN of the LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) user. Follow this template:

    ```sh
    uid=<user-name>,ou=users,dc=<okta-instance-id>,dc=okta,dc=com
    ```

    For example, if your `<user-name>` is `jane@example.com` and
    your `<okta-instance-id>` is `mdb-example`, your user's
    DN is:

    ```sh
    uid=jane@example.com,ou=users,dc=mdb-example,dc=okta,dc=com
    ```

  - If you entered a User to DN Mapping,
    enter the username or email address that your mapping requires.

- Select the database access level to grant to the user.

- Click Add User.

sections.heading: Configure OneLogin for LDAP Authorization
sections.body:
> **Note:** Skip this section if you don't want to
> enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization.

The following procedure configures Atlas to authorize users who
belong to OneLogin LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) database access groups.

> **Important:** 
>
> - You must enable authentication with LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) before enabling
>   authorization.
>
> - When you enable and configure LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization, database users
>   who are only configured for LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication will no longer
>   be able to access databases.

Atlas LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization uses LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) groups to determine if users
are authorized to perform database actions.

Create separate Okta groups for each level of access that you want
to grant to users. For example,
you create one group for read access to one database, another for read
and write access, and so on.

- Navigate to your Okta Groups page by clicking
  Directory, then Groups.

- Click Add Group.

- Enter a group name, for example `db-read`.

- Click Add Group.

Assign users to groups based on the level of access
each user requires.

- Click the group you want to add users to.

- Click Manage People.

- Add users to the group, and then click Save.

The bind user must have `Read Only Administrator` privileges to
authorize users against specific Okta groups and to perform LDAP
searches. To assign the bind user `Read Only Administrator`
privileges:

- Navigate to your Okta Administrators page by clicking
  Security, then Administrators.

- Click Add Administrator.

- Search for your bind user, then select the
  Read Only Administrator role.

- Click Add Administrator.

sections.heading: Configure Atlas for LDAP Authorization
sections.body:
> **Note:** Skip this section if you don't want to
> enable LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization.

The following procedure adds the OneLogin database access
groups to Atlas and enables database user authorization in
Atlas:

Add each of the Okta database groups you created to Atlas.
Members of groups that you add are authorized to perform database actions
granted to the group.

- Click  Add New Database User.

* Click LDAP Group, and then enter the full DN of the
  group containing your database users, even if you enabled
  User to DN Mapping. Follow this template:

  ```sh
  cn=<group-name>,ou=groups,dc=<okta-instance-id>,dc=okta,dc=com
  ```

  For example, if your `<group-name>` is `db-read` and your
  `<okta-instance-id>` is `mdb-example`, your bind user's DN is:

  ```sh
  cn=db-read,ou=groups,dc=mdb-example,dc=okta,dc=com
  ```

- Select the database access level to grant to users in this group.

- Click Add User.

When a user attempts to perform an action, Atlas executes the
[LDAP query template](manual/reference/configuration-options)
to obtain the LDAP groups to which the authenticated user belongs.
Atlas permits the action if the query returns at least one group
that is authorized to perform the action. Atlas does not permit
the action if the query returns no groups that are authorized to
perform the action.

Atlas substitutes the authenticated username in the `{USER}`
placeholder when it runs the query. The query is relative to the host
specified in Server Hostname.

The formatting for the query must conform to [RFC4515](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc4515).

If you want to identify what group a user is a member of, you can use
the following Query Template:

```sh
ou=groups,dc=<okta-instance-id>,dc=okta,dc=com?dn?sub?(&(objectClass=groupofUniqueNames)(uniqueMember={USER}))
```

> **Note:** Other query templates may also work. Using the default template of
> `{USER}?memberOf?base` may result in longer search times.

Wait for Atlas to deploy your changes. Atlas verifies that
your clusters can connect to, authenticate with, and query your LDAP
server using the configuration details that you provide.

sections.heading: Connect to your Cluster Using mongosh
sections.body:
The following procedure verifies that LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication (and LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)
authorization, if enabled) is configured correctly:

> **Note:** When LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authentication is enabled, database users must override
> the following parameters in the connection string for their
> clients:
>
> - `authSource` must be `$external`
>
> - `authenticationMechanism` must be `PLAIN`

Use [mongosh](mongodb-shell) to connect to your cluster. To copy the connection string:

- Click Connect.

- Click LDAP, and then click Copy.

- Paste and edit the string with your User DN and password.

> **Note:** Connect to your cluster with a user's full DN if User to DN
> Mapping is not enabled.

sections.heading: Troubleshoot LDAP Connection Issues
sections.body:
> **Note:** In Okta, the bind user must have `Read Only Administrator`
> privileges to perform LDAP searches. [Make sure that your bind
> user has these privileges](#assign-privileges-to-the-bind-user)
> before running `ldapsearch`.

Use `ldapsearch` to determine if the query template you
configured Atlas to use returns user DNs the way you expect. The
query template may not be returning the correct user DNs if LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol)
authentication works but LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) authorization doesn't.

Use the following `ldapsearch` template:

```sh
ldapsearch -H 'ldaps://<okta-instance-id>.ldap.okta.com' -D "<bind-user-dn>" -w "<bind-user-pwd>" -b 'ou=groups,dc=<okta-instance-id>,dc=okta,dc=com' '(&(objectClass=groupofUniqueNames)(uniqueMember=<bind-user-dn or group-dn>))
```

For example, if your `bind-user-dn` is
`uid=jane@example.com,ou=users,dc=mdb-example,dc=okta,dc=com`
and your `<okta-instance-id>` is `mdb-example`, use the
following command:

```sh
ldapsearch -H 'ldaps://mdb-example.ldap.okta.com' -D "uid=jane@example.com,dc=mdb-example,dc=okta,dc=com" -w "REDACTED" -b 'ou=groups,dc=mdb-example,dc=okta,dc=com' '(&(objectClass=groupofUniqueNames)(uniqueMember=uid=jane@example.com,ou=users,dc=mdb-example,dc=okta,dc=com))'
```

> **Note:** Other query templates may also work. Using the default template of
> `{USER}?memberOf?base` may result in longer search times.
